comments.title: Intro to Spirituality for Materialists and Request for Resources on Giants
comments.content:
Good Evening,

I hope everyone's New Year is already heading the way you would like, and keeps on doing so. Also, thanks very much to those who responded about World Tree resources two weeks ago, I am happily digging into them - especially the "Survive the Jive" mini-documentary that an anonymous commenter shared, so thanks whoever you are!

To Share: As my first "real" post (rather than re-posting a comment from here) of the year to keep up with my resolution to blog weekly, I've written up The Open-Minded Materialist's Gentle Introduction to Spirituality (https://jpowellrussell.com/#open-minded_materialists_gentle_introduction_to_spirituality). It came to me that I a still in a "sweet spot" where I recall what it was like looking at the world as a materialist, but I've changed my views enough to explain why I don't anymore, and I thought that might come in handy for folks unhappy with the meaning in their lives, but unsure about how to approach spirituality. I doubt anyone here will get much from what it has to say, but I'd very much welcome any thoughts on how to make it better, either via email (at the foot of the post), or on the dreamwidth comment thread here: https://jprussell.dreamwidth.org/2505.html

To Ask: For JMG or the commentariat: any recomendations for works, themes for meditation, or other avenues for exploring the Indo-European tradition of giants/titans/jotnar/etc? From what I've found so far, looking at them /only/ as "like Gods, but bad" or "representations of primitive/chaotic nature forces" or the like is overly simplistic, but likewise seeing them as "just another category of Gods" also doesn't seem to square with the mythology.

As always, thanks very much to JMG and those who read and comment for all that you share.

To any who will have them, I put forth my blessings and best wishes,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Intro to Spirituality for Materialists and Request for Resources on Giants
replies.content:
Welsh giants are kind of an oddity. Einigan, Bran the Blessed, and (in some versions) Hu Gadarn are giants in terms of size, but they don't have the oppositional quality that (say) ettins or Titans have. Bran's an especially good example -- his parents and his siblings are all normal size, but he's a giant. Why? The Mabinogion gives no clue.

According to Geoffrey of Monmouth, there were giants all over the British Isles before Brutus and his band of exiled Trojans arrived there; Corineus, who gave Cornwall its name, was particularly famous for wrestling giants and beating them. Arthur vanquished his share of giants, too. No doubt those stories all reach back toward Indo-European antiquity, but they've been filtered through so many cultural filters I'm not sure they'll do you any good.

replies.title: Re: Intro to Spirituality for Materialists and Request for Resources on Giants
replies.content:
To borrow a JMG-ism: Hmmm! I had not considered a practical/ritual reflection of the often-encountered "inside-outside the enclosure" dynamic. That is certainly worth thinking about/exploring more.

Incidentally, I watched Jackson Crawford's video about how innangard and utangard don't really show up in the corpus of Old Norse literature, and I got to wondering how the terms came to be so prominent in modern Heathenry, and I found this article, which was actually written in response to Crawford's video: https://wildhunt.org/2020/01/column-innangard-and-utangard-problematic-roots-of-heathen-dualism.html

It's got a pretty woke axe to grind, but it does so in a calmer and more considered way than you often find, and I was thankful for someone pointing to "this is the work that likely introduced the term" (which seems to have been Gronbech's "Culture of the Teutons", which is sitting on my "to read" pile).

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Intro to Spirituality for Materialists and Request for Resources on Giants
replies.content:
By golly Jeff, you got me to click a Wildhunt link! ;)

Yeah I totally see the woke axe to grind in that article, but I do suspect the author is making some valid points. AFAIK, Folkish Heathenry has very little to do with the way ancient polytheists thought or conducted their daily lives, and much do with a hungover reading of Nietzsche, Julius Evola, maybe some Ariosophy, and a smattering of 19th century European romanticist tidbits.

The one area I'd really disagree with the author on is his assertion that mythical stories of a hero god (like Thor, Hercules, or Indra) traversing to the "outer realms" to battle giants, monsters, or demons, carries no spiritual significance. What a doozy of a claim. Those stories obviously do (as do most ancient myths)! And I'd say it doesn't take a lot of brain power to see these stories as soul-struggle allegories. These sort of takes aren't surprising though, as Woke and Folkish Neopagans alike tend to put spirituality a distant second to their pet political ideology, if even considering spirituality at all.

BTW, nice blog post. I'm pretty sure I'll share my two cents at some point soonish.

replies.title: Re: Intro to Spirituality for Materialists and Request for Resources on Giants

Heh, I hadn't heard of Wildhunt before this, but I'm guessing it's got a reputation consistent with the vibes I was picking up.

That being said, yeah, I agree: he had some valid points that had the misfortune of dragging around a lot of ideological baggage (and as you say, you get the same amount of baggage from the folkish side of things).

My takeaway was something like "okay, we don't really have evidence that 'innangard' and 'utangard' were named concepts that were part of a formal framework, that likely came from the 19th century, anad so it does likely have much of the biases you might expect, but there's clearly something to the idea that one of the things happening in the Norse myths is a contrast and conflict between normal life and the wilds outside, so it's going to far to say those ideas are pure 19th century fabrications."

And thank you! I look forward to hearing them.

replies.title: Re: Intro to Spirituality for Materialists and Request for Resources on Giants
replies.content:
Hindu mythology has plenty of giants, but they are rather different from the giants of northern Europe. Most of the giants of ancient India belonged to the race of demonic humanoids called “rakshasas”: while most rakshasas were of normal human dimensions, many could change their form and size at will – although some were giants all the time (my favourite being Gatotkacha, son of the Pandu prince Bhimsen and a rakshasa mother, who on is last day of battle in the Mahabharat war destroyed one “akshauhini” (218,700 warriors) of the enemy and could only be killed by a single-use “weapon of last resort” by the supreme archer Karna). Another category of giants is monsters who became so as a curse. And the last category are gods and demigods (such as the monkey-god Hanuman, son of the Wind God, who was able to change size from the atomic to the colossal at will – and was able to leap from India to Sri Lanka in a single bound [kind of a combo of “Ant Man” and “Superman”]). Hope this provides some insight – or at least entertainment, Jeff!

Ron M

- They are primeval, not only predating but also parenting the 'civilised' gods (and most stuff as well)

Thank you! "Meditate on the primary myths" is always a good answer, and as you say, is likely the best answer here.

What got me wondering about this is that I just read Maria Kvilhaug's The Goddess Idhunn in Myth and Poetry, and it talks extensively about what symbolic and/or ritual role Thjazi (which she translates as "Slave Binder") was playing in kidnapping the source of the Gods' renewing life. In doing so, she talks a bit about other giants in Norse myths, and some of those tied into some elements discussed in some of the comparative stuff I've been checking out lately (like the video I mentioned in my original comment).

I think your point that they are somehow not "relatable", sometimes even to the point of dangerousness is an interesting one and worth some meditation. Maybe consciousnesses that are for one reason or another less kindly-disposed and/or more indifferent toward helping humans on our spiritual path than the Gods, but of a similar level of power/development. It's a big cosmos, and not everything in it necessarily wants to be our friend!

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Intro to Spirituality for Materialists and Request for Resources on Giants
replies.content:
Hi JP,
The giants (jotuns/ettins/etc.) have been a source of intrigue and meditations for me over the past couple years. I've found it helpful to read norse and greek myths and compare and contrast the non-human extra-pantheon beings. There is quite a bit of overlap and I find it interesting how much intermingling there is between the Olympians/Aesir and the giants/titans. There are multiple of the inner-pantheon gods that are progeny (on at least one side) of a non-pantheon being.

I've wondered if the separation is less about biology/morphology and more about society/caste/status. In Peru I learned that the native peoples were and are referred to as Quechua, and that the more famous Incas (who are thought of as "vanished") were really just the privileged city-dwelling classes of Quechua who "disappeared" when the empire collapsed, not because they all died, but because their social class was eliminated. I've wondered if the classes of beings in these myths are similar - perhaps Aesir are just the political, power-seeking subgroup of beings. In some myths, I connect more with giants than the Aesir. Perhaps as part of the rural working-class, I'm inclined to root for the outlying underdogs.

I've recently read two fiction books in which I found lovely meditation fodder and interesting views on the relationships between the Gods and other beings: Circe by Madeline Miller and The Witch's Heart by Genevieve Gornichec (covering the relationship between Angrboda and Loki.)

Wishing you well!

Thanks! That example of the Qechua/Inca being defined by their social position/class opens up a whole host of other points of comparison to consider, so I'll have to go check that out.

And yeah, Greek and Norse myth are the two bodies of myth I'm most familiar with (though I'm chipping away at Insular Celtic via my Druid Work), and the similarities and contrasts between the jotnar and the titans is always interesting. I suspect if I want to get really serious about Indo-European comparative mythology, I'm going to have to tackle the massive and intimidating body of Hindu lore. Too much stuff to learn, too little time!

Cheers,

replies.title: Re: Intro to Spirituality for Materialists and Request for Resources on Giants
replies.content:
"looking at them /only/ as "like Gods, but bad" or "representations of primitive/chaotic nature forces" or the like is overly simplistic, but likewise seeing them as "just another category of Gods" also doesn't seem to square with the mythology."

Maybe if you remove "primitive/chaotic", "just another category of Gods" makes sense? Humans make up only a small fraction of biological life which was possibly much more obvious in past times than it is today. Why should only humans have gods?

If I imagine looking at the mighty serpent of the river Rhine as it was in past times or a quiet sail through the many small islands of the Danish "south sea" - nature holds a lot of sweetness also for humans. But it can be hard and deadly (just as it can be for any other living being). That's possibly the reason why female giants tend to be beautiful and even friendly from time to time and male giants not so? Generally, the giants are maybe "just another category of gods", but a category that is just not so much concerned with the well-being of humans like the Aesir. That doesn't mean they are chaotic and primitive, but if you are sitting on the receiving end of their lack of interest in human affairs, it may easily being interpreted as such.

I see the eternal struggle between giants and the other gods as a symbol of our struggle to maximize the experience of the sweet aspects of nature and minimize the impact of the things that are not so sweet without tilting the balance so far that everything breaks. Maybe Ragnarök is the story to show that this does not *always* work out...?

So ok, no reading resources from me, just my few cents. I have to say I am no great scholar of heathen mythology and there's still so much to read and meditate on. As we're at it - do you (or anybody else) know about resources about the dwarfs and what they are or might be?

Greetings,
Nachtgurke

replies.title: Re: Intro to Spirituality for Materialists and Request for Resources on Giants
replies.content:
Thank you for this!

I suppose I ought to have been more careful in my phrasing. When I said "just another category of Gods", I was referring mostly to the subset of modern Heathens who actively worship the Jotnar and other entities usually seen as "Bad" like Nidhoggr. For one example, Raven Kaldera calls this group the "Rokkr" and sees them as the Gods of chaotic/harsh things that folks don't always like, but that are a natural part of the world, and so deserve worship and respect.

I think your take of "Gods, but not necessarily for humans" is far more interesting. I'm also going to need to meditate on the thought that most of the Giants that enter into positive relationships with humans or the Gods are female (the only male counter-examples that come to mind are Mimir and Loki, but well, Loki's complicated). The idea that Ragnarok is a myth about trying to have all of the good of nature with none of the bad also sounds like a fruitful theme for meditation.

As for dwarves, why, I'm so glad you asked, as I just found a few sources of interest myself:
1) The Hidden History of Dwarves and Elves by Claude Lecouteux - I started reading this one before my year of "read only books by the dead" and had to set it aside for 2022, and I'm going to get back into it soon, but I gave it a somewhat thorough skim - Lecouteux argues that Elves and Dwarves are mostly ancestors, with Elves being the shining, radiant spirit bodies of the dead, while Dwarves are their broken physical bodies. I believe there's more nuance to it, but as I said, I just skimmed through to get the main ideas and then had to put it down for a while.

2) The Duergarbok by Susannah Ravenswing - This is more on the "gnosis" side of things, but with lots of reference to the Norse mythology we have about the duergar. It is firmly in the camp of "these are divine beings that cover a lesser-understood part of the world, but are worthy of worship and respect" (Raven Kaldera is quoted fairly extensively and it comes from the same press that puts out his books). Haven't read this yet, I think it will be worth reading.

3) Seed of Yggdrasil by Maria Kvilhaug - Another book that I haven't yet read, but I just read another book by her about Idhunn, and in it she referenced some of her arguments about what the dwarves are doing mythologically, which she goes into in more detail here. It seems that she argues something like the dwarves were necessary (but limited) templates for the Gods to shape and manifest in the material world before they developed better ways of doing so. The idea that came to me reading it was of "astral vehicles", but I'm going to have to think about it more.

Thanks again for your response, and I hope these give you some interesting avenues to go down,
Jeff

comments.title: Anger Statement or Affirmation
comments.content:
I've been struggling with anger for a year. I used the DA 7-Fold Wheel of Thought to examine it and an idea popped into my mind along the lines of "Actively choosing and owning my own life is the solution to my anger."

The anger stems from my not being active in my life and letting circumstances, inertia, fear/anxiety, or other people rule me. This year I'm going to build my will and take action every day to get myself out of this.

I'm already doing a different affirmation this year, but I am wondering if I could use this statement whenever I feel the anger coming on: "Actively choosing & owning my own life is the solution to my anger."

Do you think that could work, or do you think having a statement that says the word 'anger' in it might affirm my anger? I jouraled 20k words last year. That's a lot! Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Anger Statement or Affirmation
replies.content: That's a perfectly workable affirmation. The anger is real, and it's yours; by including that in your affirmation you're not changing that -- but you're defining it as an issue to be solved, and you can then proceed to solve it.

replies.title: Re: Anger Statement or Affirmation
replies.content:
Not sure if I should comment, but the part about "owning my own life" surprises me. Who owns his own life?

Redirect the energy behind anger to the things you care about.

Maybe simplification can help. Choose three things you care about and make a point of ignoring the rest. "Own" three things. Do not extend a finger for the rest.

I tried this when I was drowned in tasks and was pleasantly surprised at the result.

replies.title: Re: Anger Statement or Affirmation
replies.content: Philosophically? We don't. I relate to cs2 in that feeling lack of agency in one's own life can produce a lot of anger. For me it felt like a transfer of responsibility, reminding me that it's me who is the one that makes choices helps. A similar affirmation worked. I believe your situation is a little different, which seems about not feeling responsible for more than you can handle.

comments.title: Autism and Reincarnation
comments.content:
Long time reader, recent practitioner here. I'm about 3 months in working with the Druid Magic Handbook. Interestingly, daily practise seems to have made it quite easy for me to also adopt a regular writing habit - as well as other benefits.

I've appreciated your occasional comments on Aspergers Syndrome, as I am of similar neural wiring. I'm curious as to whether, based on your exploration of past lives, how autism/aspergers relates to that. Is it tied to a specific incarnation? Is it something that repeats with each? Does it ebb and flow?

Thanks

Stephen

replies.title: Re: Autism and Reincarnation
replies.content:
I can't speak to anyone else's experience, of course, but my last incarnation ended in a head-on car crash in which I was in the passenger seat -- no, I wasn't wearing a seatbelt, either. (This was in the very late 1950s, when many people neglected those.) If I'd survived, I would have had serious head injuries and brain damage from the impact with the windshield...and the neurological problems I have in this incarnation are oddly like the sorts of things you'd expect someone to have if they survived traumatic brain injury.

As far as I can tell, I wasn't on the autism spectrum at all in any of the incarnations of which I have clear memories, and -- like a lot of Aspies -- I've had a gradual improvement of symptoms over the course of this life. So I'm guessing that it's repercussion from the manner of my last death. It wouldn't surprise me if the steep increase in autism spectrum diagnoses in recent decades is a function of the ongoing death rate from car crashes...

replies.title: Re: Autism and Reincarnation
replies.content:
(Stephen again)

That's interesting. Thank you.

Does this apply to other forms of trauma I wonder? If one is not able to process, for example, psychological trauma in one lifetime, would that mean needing to deal with it in the future somehow?

I've hypothesized myself that autism could be some kind of epigenetic trauma response - perhaps if society becomes sufficiently dysfunctional then more unconventional thinkers are needed to help dig out of the mess it's in. Perhaps more relevant to the kind of societies humans evolved in than the ones we find ourselves in today.

But equally it could just be all the lead. I'm certainly not pursuing a career in the sciences to try to find out.

comments.title: Octagon Society - Law of Acceptance
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

First Question
--------------
I'm working my way through the Octagon Society instructional papers and am busy with the Law of Acceptance (0/8). My first question is about the mechanics of the Breath of Acceptance.

The text states: "Imagine this person standing in front of you during that period of time between your in breath and your out breath."

The next instruction is: "... telling the other person, place or thing, as we imagine them during our meditation, that we know there were reasons for the things they did."

Does this mean that one imagines the person during the pause between inbreath and outbreath, carry on breathing and then start the conversation in your head with this person?

Or does it mean one imagines the person during the pause between inbreath and outbreath, have the conversation in your head with this person and then carry on breathing?


Second question
---------------
The text states: "Begin by accepting the fact you are an individualized expression of the Divine."

I'm having trouble with this because I have not figured out what "Divine" means to me. I know at the start you mention that one needs some kind of belief in divine powers to do this work and I'm trying to do that at the same time as doing the OS work. At the moment the druid path (as far as I understand it) feels the most natural to me and I use those deities in my SOP work. I have bought The Druid Path and am about to start reading it.

I have also started to read A Guide for the Perplexed (have reached chapter 4) and so far everything makes sense to me. So at the moment I'm telling myself that the Divine is that entity that can instigate or create factors x y and z in the world.

I was wondering if you or any of the commentariat could help me with some ideas of how to think about the divine in a simple but practical manner so that I can carry on with the OS work because I really need to do this. It feels like my last hope of improving my mental health.

Thanks

Lavender Nauseous Newt

replies.title: Re: Octagon Society - Law of Acceptance
replies.content:
1) In this form of breathwork, you only visualize during the pause. The first few breaths, just concentrate on the image of the person. In subsequent breaths, carry on the conversation. You can divide it up by breaths:

breathe in --> you say something --> breathe out
breathe in --> the person responds --> breathe out
breathe in --> you say something else --> breathe out

and so on.

2) It's okay not to have a clear sense of what the Divine is. Just accept that there is a power in the cosmos greater than you, which wants to help you heal and has power to do so. You can certainly fill in the details as you go, but that basic acceptance is enough.

replies.title: Re: Octagon Society - Law of Acceptance
replies.content:
You wrote:

I'm having trouble with this because I have not figured out what "Divine" means to me.

The Arch Druid report on The Renewal of Relegion was useful to me. I'll quote it here:

It so happens that a large minority of human beings—up to a third, depending on the survey—report having at least one experience, at some point in their lives, that appears to involve contact with a disembodied intelligent being. Many of these experiences are spontaneous; others seem to be fostered by religious practices such as prayer, meditation, and ritual. Any number of causes have been proposed for these experiences, but I’d like to ask my readers to set aside the issue of causation for the moment and pay attention to the raw data of experience. There’s a crucial difference between the question “Does x happen?” and the question “Why does x happen?”—a difference of basic logical categories—and it’s a fruitful source of confusion and error to confuse them.

Whether they are caused by autohypnosis, undiagnosed schizophrenia, archetypes of the collective unconscious, the real existence of gods and spirits, or something else, these experiences happen to a great many people, they have done so as far back as records go, and religion is the traditional human response to them. If nobody had ever had the experience of encountering a god, an angel, a saint, an ancestor, a totem spirit, or what have you, it’s probably safe to say that we would not have religions. Human beings under ordinary conditions encounter two kinds or, if you will, worlds of experience: one that’s composed of things that can be seen, heard, smelled, tasted, and touched, which we might as well call the biosphere, and one composed of things that can be thought, felt, willed, and imagined, which we can call the noosphere (from Greek nous, “mind”). The core theory held by religions everywhere is that there is a third world, which we can call the theosphere, and that this is what breaks through into human consciousness in religious experience.

Last week there was a question of if maintaining an emergency fund is a magic act that will in itself prevent emergencies. If this is so -

2. How does this relate to the Law of Flow? It seems like that should reward just-in-time production rather than maintaining an inventory.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) I consider it wise to keep a full pantry, and a good supply of anything else you really need.

2) That's an excellent question with a somewhat complex answer. First, always rotate your stash! Just as a river has pools where the water rests for a while, your full pantry should always be in flux, with new purchases added to it and old ones being rotated out and eaten. The Law of Flow doesn't demand that every river should be a straight line rushing as fast as possible to the sea. Second, don't let keeping a reasonable supply of necessities stop you from helping other people; I recommend figuring out how much money you put into prepping, and donating an equivalent sum each year to a charity that helps those who truly need it. That way you're still part of the flow, you're just participating in it in a way that can help you weather social turbulence. Think of the way that wetlands soak up sudden downpours and let the water flow out into rivers a little at a time, preventing floods downstream; your stash can function like that.

comments.title: Elemental work
comments.content:
John,

I’ve read your book “Circles of Power” and I’m curious to learn more about elemental spirit work—specifically, what are some of the kinds of things that elemental work is good for, and what can be accomplished with it. Do you go into this sort of thing in further detail in other works?

replies.title: Re: Elemental work
replies.content: No, I don't discuss it in detail in other books, and that's deliberate. If you want to work with elementals, or with the elements more generally, the way to figure things out is to meditate on the elements, then do a series of elemental pathworkings, and then meditate on everything that you experience during the pathworkings. The reason for this is that each of us has a unique personal balance of elements, and so it's very rare to find two people who can do the same things with the elemental kingdoms. So the thing to do is to find out what the elements will do with and for you, and then begin elemental ritual workings and let the results guide you.

comments.title: Taking the Plutonian out of Comics
comments.content:
Good Morning JMG,

I missed posting on the comments last Wednesday but I am greatly looking forward to the current series of posts. I'd wondered what became of the spell for modernity concept that appeared here sometime in the past couple years.

I also finished your book on Pluto (it takes me a while to get to things!). It occurred to me that comics might be a Plutonian phenomenon- they got big in the 1930's-60's, ossified after that, and are facing industry-wide collapse. They also split the page into smaller images in order to release the energy of movement, splitting the sense of space to create a sense of time. Superhero comics, in particular, fit this mold the best.

On the other hand, words and stylized images go back to the mid 18th century, along with serialized storytelling, which would indicate a Uranian influence.

While the US superhero comic industry is dying an unfortunately well-deserved and rapid death, foreign comics (especially Japanese and Korean) are bigger than ever - a single issue of a single manga title outsold the entire US comic industry last year. What could make the difference, astrologically speaking, between the two? And would you have any recommendations for inviting a less plutonian influence in graphic storytelling?

Of the top of my head - avoiding the superhero genre, or some kind of heavily illustrated novel instead of comics proper.

.
.
.
My decidedly non-superhero comic can be found [here](https://www.patreon.com/posts/etherwood-1-74944566).

replies.title: Re: Taking the Plutonian out of Comics
replies.content: I think you've already put your finger on it. I haven't read a great deal in the manga genre, but the ones I've read -- "The Girl from the Other Side," "The Ancient Magus' Bride," and "The Elder Sister-like One" -- are far more Neptunian than either Uranian or Plutonian, with a vivid sense of fantasy and a dreamlike capacity to suspend disbelief. That's not surprising, because as Pluto fades out Neptune, the Lord of the Great Deep, is taking on greater importance. Superheroes have had their day -- the whole concept, fresh and fascinating in the 1930s, is weighed down with more clichés now than Superman himself could lift -- and the whole violent dualism of the superhero era, with overblown heroes battling equally overblown villains, is equally overdone. There are still countless stories that can be told, and told well, in sequences of vivid images with word balloons and captions; they're just different stories than the ones that dominated the Age of Superheroes. I look forward to seeing some of them.

comments.title: Odin
comments.content:
You have said before that Norse paganism is not one of your specialties. However I have no idea where to ask for authoritative advice about it, in the way we can ask you about aspects of Celtic paganism and occultism. I am only aware of subreddits and those seem like seekers with a few months to years of experience trying to advise each other.

I think my question could be more general, though, about how we understand deities based on a blend of experience/intuition, surviving texts where they are present, lore among contemporary believers, and influential elements of pop culture.

Among the latter, I hear quite different things said about Odin by those who primarily focus on other gods alongside those who are interested in Norse paganism in a more academic way, as opposed to those who feel called by Odin.

The first group view him as more dangerous (favoured by the aristocracy historically, his death and swindler/trickster aspects) and emphasise that Thor was the god of everyday people and is more "what you see is what you get".

I am one of those who feel drawn to Odin, and have been for some years. I have just "gone with it" as I had learnt was the way from Pagan literature, and was glad to do so. The way some of those who feel called describe it, they speak of it as if there was not much choice. That is one way of looking at it, though I also feel like there are various features in his story which make him an obvious choice for me. They also often, as I do, seem to see him mostly in his Wanderer, Wise One aspect rather than warrior, though they know the warrior is present below the surface.

Considering that almost everyone interested in paganism and polytheism will have read Tolkien, or at least watched movie adaptations, I think there is an inevitable influence of an idea along the lines of "Gandalf with an edge"

And as many people these days are more highly educated than in the Viking age, they travel frequently and can hear about things happening anywhere in the world via the internet, he seems like a relevant god for a lot more people now.

I would be very interested in more authoritative comments on these aspects of Odin from you, JMG, if possible, and from anyone who has been involved with Norse paganism for a long time. I recall you've commented before on the often malign influence of Loki, and how many of his worshippers whom you've encoutered have been silly, messed up or naive. I paraphrase, it is a long time since I read those posts.


Thank you again for this space.

I had a second, separate question, but in typing it out here, a reasonable answer came to me, which you would probably approve of. (Read fewer posts by angry people on the internet, and focus more on the spirituality that has previously helped me feel calmer about the issue.) Sometimes you help without actively having to answer.

replies.title: Re: Odin
replies.content:
I certainly can't claim to be authoritative, and I'm firmly in the "seeker with a few years of experience" category, so take with as much salt as seems appropriate to you.

Odin/Woden is the God whose myths I felt the greatest draw toward for as long as I can remember, and so when I first decided to give polytheist worship a try, he was where I started. Since then, I've had multiple positive experiences, and keeping on with reading historical sources, modern speculation about them, comparative material, books meant for practicing Heathens, as well as the old fashioned 20th century occultism our host has turned me on to has all helped flesh out, contextualize, and to some degree guide those experiences.

What has been most central for me, however, has been meditation. Every strong religious experience I've had (of Odin/Woden or otherwise) has been brought on by meditation. So, if that's not already a part of your practice, you might consider it.

More prosaically, the single book that has done the most for me to both show where the scary stuff came from, but also put it into a less scary context, has been The One-Eyed God: Odin and the (Indo-) Germanic Mannerbunde by Kris Kershaw. If you haven't had a chance to read it and already are interested in/know a good deal about Odin, I think you'll find it fascinating, and if you combine with meditation, it provides a lot of themes.

Cheers, and good luck on your search,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Odin
replies.content:
I think I've yet to find a single contemporary Heathen book I'm wholly satisfied with, and that's become even less true as I've gotten more interested in the kind of occultism our host teaches and less interested in strict reconstructionism, that being said, I put together this (mostly) Heathen list for resources on practice over in the Sane Polytheism group:
https://sanepolytheism.dreamwidth.org/3051.html

Overall, I've had to adopt a "read widely, think hard about what makes sense, and then pray, meditate, and divine on how to move forward." As such, I've tried to hit a wide variety of sources, especially those modern ones that might have the arguments and agendas you talk about.

A few things I didn't mention there that are more specifically maybe-useful for Heathens:
1) Teutonic Religion & Teutonic Magic by Kveldulf Gunnarsson - I have to especially give these books a shout out because their "pathworkings" (guided meditations) played an important role in my own spiritual development. Also has some helpfully hands-on stuff, like step by step instructions to make your own mead horn.
2) A Modern Guide to Heathenry by Galina Krasskova - Krasskova has gotten involved in her share of the online political arguments you talk about, and has some personal practices and beliefs that I'm not sure I'm comfortable with, but her writing is extremely even-handed, and she takes prayer, devotion, and ritual very seriously. She also has some good insights on Heathen cosmology.
3) Seed of Yggdrasil by Maria Kvilhaug - I've only skimmed this book, but I just read another, less general one (The Goddess Idhunn in Myth and Poetry) by Kvilhaug, and I found it extremely thought-provoking, and I look forward to digging into this mammoth of a book. Most useful thing here is that she translates proper names into English wherever possible, such as "Thjazi" as "Slave Binder". I'm not sure I buy everything she puts forward, but as I said, thought-provoking.
4) Heathen Soul Lore vol. I and II by Winnifred Hodge Rose - Mrs. Rose stops by here sometimes, and I found these books through our host's recommendation. I haven't read them yet since I spent the last year only reading books by the dead, but from what I've seen on her blog, she is kind, thoughtful, and with very deep insights into spiritual practice.
5) And, if you happen to be interested in Anglo-Saxon Heathenry in particular, everything by Stephen Pollington is great. It's much more in the academic style, but I get the feeling he's writing it from a Heathen viewpoint he's very careful to keep subtle so as to make the works academically respectable.

Also, though I don't much like videos, there are some folks with interesting and helpful ideas who are mostly sharing them on youtube:
6) The "Survive the Jive" youtube channel - I only found this in the past few weeks after a commenter recommended it to me, but I've been enjoying it. Very focused on comparative mythology and historical reconstruction, but from the standpoint of a practicing polytheist.
7) The "Nordic Animism" youtube channel - very different take, this is a Danish academic of a decidedly Left outlook, but who approaches things from an "animist" perspective informed by his work with mostly African-derived indigenous traditions in Brazil.
8) Dr. Scott Shell's youtube channel - another academic, but a Runologist (so, mostly a linguist) rather than an anthropologist, Dr. Shell's focus is on continental Heathenry, but he posts plenty of stuff relevant those interested in Norse or Anglo-Saxon matters as well. Very calm and reasonable, careful with his sources, but willing to draw some conclusions of his own and is also a practicing Heathen that takes the magical and religious sides of things seriously.

Lastly, neither book nor youtube, the "Germanic Mythology" website is an ENORMOUS trove of stuff to check out that I've barely scratched: http://www.germanicmythology.com/index.html

Hope these help!
Jeff

West/Water: Osiris
North/Earth: Nepthys
Spirit Below: Atum
Spirit Above: Ra

Are there any other versions out there that people use? Something which includes Djehuty/Hathor? replies.title: Re: Egyptian Sphere of Protection
replies.content:
You can create your own set of correspondences to the Sphere of Protection -- it's not at all required that you use one that someone else came up with. John Gilbert used to encourage people to come up with their own. The Egyptian version you cited is one that I came up with very nearly on the spur of the moment, and of course it's strongly influenced by the Ennead of Heliopolis, which is the aspect of Egyptian religion that got most attention from the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn. You can certainly do one with Thoth and Hathor if you prefer. For example, you could use:

East: Horus
South: Ma'at
West: Djehuti
North: Hathor
Below: Osiris
Above: Ra

This includes Horus and Thoth with their consorts, and then the Ra-and-Osiris polarity that has so important a role in New Kingdom theology. You could replace Ma'at with Seshat if you like, since the latter's also strongly associated with Thoth, and you could also replace Osiris and Ra with any number of polarized deities -- say, Geb and Nut. There's plenty of room for flexibility and variation.

replies.title: Re: Egyptian Sphere of Protection

On the searching:

Dreamwidth's native search function (at the bottom of the column that appears on the left on a computer, and way, waaaaay at the bottom on mobile) includes a little checkbox for "include comments". It's still not great, but it seems to be fairly thorough, and you can use quotation marks to help find specific phrases (for example, "sphere of protection" rather than it searching for any instance of sphere OR of OR protection, which is the default without quotes). Oh, and you have to be logged into dreamwidth to search.

The other option is to go to the search engine of your choice and include with the word(s) you want to find site:ecosophia.dreamwidth.org (without the quotes for this), but I've found that neither Google, nor Duckduck Go, nor Brave have a very good index of the site, so it'll miss a lot of stuff.

Hope this helps if you find yourself looking for something else!
Jeff

comments.title: Hand gestures
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Some questions on hand gestures from me:

Firstly, what's the significance of the two-finger gesture often seen used by saints and Jesus in medieval paintings, etc? (Like this: https://i.pinimg.com/736x/77/d3/04/77d3044b99fc1999a5273473ba02f55a--medieval-symbols-medieval-art.jpg) Sometimes the ring finger and little finger are folded into the palm, sometimes they are touching the thumb.

Secondly, on a related note, do swearing gestures have any sort of actual cursing effect, beyond causing insult to the target? (Such as the middle finger gesture)

Thirdly, do you know of any resources which list the occult significance and meaning of hand gestures? It's a difficult thing to look up, you just get loads of junk and weirdness.

Thanks,
Mr. Crow

replies.title: Re: Hand gestures
replies.content:
1) The two fingers refer to the two natures of Christ, divine and human, in Christian theology. The thumb and the other two fingers, folded into the palm or joined, refer to the Trinity.

2) Nope, no more than the words they symbolize.

3) I wish I did! You're right that there's a lot of stupidity out there.

replies.title: Re: Hand gestures
replies.content: My understanding is that flipping the bird was originally understood as invoking the phallus in its apotropaic capacity...

replies.title: Re: Hand gestures
replies.content:
1) is absolutely correct for Catholic contexts. For the Orthodox form, with the thumb touching the ring finger I found this explanation:

"When the priest blesses us, he forms the Greek letters IC XC, that is, Jesus Christ, with the fingers of his hand. This means that through the priest our Lord Jesus Christ Himself blesses us. Therefore, we must receive the blessing of the clergy with reverence."

https://www.orthodoxphotos.com/readings/LGOT/blessing.shtml

replies.title: Re: Hand gestures
replies.content:
On 3), a few things that might help:

a) The "Kuji-in" are a set of nine hand gestures used in Japanese Shinto/Buddhist practice for purification and banishing
b) Guido von List developed a system of hand gestures to represent each Rune in his idiosyncratic "Armanen" set of Runes, which Edred Thorsson expanded/adapted to the Elder Futhark set of Runes.
c) Some folks use the Hindu/Buddhist term "mudra" as a generic term for hand gestures with magical/ritual significance, so it might be a more useful search term. That being said, I first found that usage in Isaac Bonewits's Real Magic, which is not very well thought of around here, and it doesn't have many further pointers, except to suggest that Gurdjieff and his followers wrote some about mudra.

I hope that some of the above helps you out!
Jeff

replies.title: (no subject)

I'll take these out of order.

If it's a deeply rooted hot button issue, it's going to take a while to unpack. Your mental picture of your aura sounds quite tolerably accurate, and suggests you have some kind of unresolved emotional trauma that the meditation stirred up. That's normal; your task now is to keep working at it until the emotional energies finish surfacing and you can deal with them.

If your left leg is longer than your right, you probably have a whole mess of muscular tensions caused by having to maintain your balance with uneven legs. Tensions like that often surface during meditation.

If you're feeling like a cluttered mess, good. Nearly all human beings are cluttered messes. Now that you're aware of that condition, just keep going with your meditation and your other practices, and the mess will unravel a step at a time.

replies.title: Re: Santa Muerte
replies.content: I'm far from an expert on that particular subject, but my best guess is that yes, the Pretty Girl is an indigenous goddess who has found new worshippers. The accounts I've read suggest that she's both loving and dangerous -- those two terms aren't contradictory, after all!  The fact that she picked up her first worshippers on the fringes of society should come as no surprise -- most new religions do. (Christianity certainly did, back in its early days.) replies.title: Re: Santa Muerte
replies.content:
I'd like to offer my thoughts on this question. I've been worshiping Santa Muerte for a little while now. She does bring fast results, and she accepts and works with pretty much anyone, good or bad. Death, as I've heard other devotees say, embraces everyone. Death is present everywhere, as all things eventually break down and "die" in some sense or other. From that kind of omnipresent, all-embracing power, one can only expect it to work with and respond to a wide variety of requests and worshipers.

She also expects regular, consistent worship. If you slack off on that, she will stop helping you, until you've made some kind of amends and resumed consistent devotion for a time. She might also cause your requests to go awry. I don't think this makes her anymore dangerous than other deities. One does not disrespect a divine being without consequences. In my experience, she's a loving mother who will discipline her wayward children, if they break their promises/commitments to her.

She is not a demon. Far from it. I used to be plagued by what I am certain is a demonic entity. I used things like vinegar dishes and the LBRP to keep the thing at bay, but it never quite went away. replies.title: Re: Interesting Post About Past Life Brain Trauma
replies.content:
1) Yes, that's typically a matter of karma. You don't need to figure it out, though it can be helpful if you do; the point of karma is that it gives you the experiences you need to shape your character, and make it a more and more perfect fit for your higher self. When your lower self and higher self are close enough, you wake up out of the dream of material incarnation and remember who you were all along -- as Zen masters put it, you see the face you had before your mother and father were born.

I've had past life memories surfacing spontaneously, as a result of daily meditation practice, for about thirty years now -- brief fragments at first, but then quite a bit more. replies.title: Re: Polytheistic compatible Renaissance Magic
replies.content:
Astrological and geomantic magic as practiced in the Renaissance were almost explicitly polytheist -- the Picatrix, the main source for Renaissance astrological magic, contains quite a bit of overtly polytheist planet-worship under a very thin veneer of Muslim monotheism. Renaissance natural magic can be worked either way. The entire body of Solomonic magic, and indeed the whole range of Renaissance and early modern grimoire magic, is out -- it's explicitly predicated on an Abrahamic worldview in which the names of the one God can be used to command all other spirits.

If a polytheist version of Renaissance magic interests you, start by familiarizing yourself with the Orphic hymns, Renaissance Hermeticism, and Renaissance astrology, and then proceed to contemporary sources such as Cornelius Agrippa and Marsilio Ficino. The Picatrix is essential -- it's the unnamed source of much of the work of both authors I've named -- and you may also find Giordano Bruno relevant. Chris Warnock's books on practical astrological magic are also very good resources.
